name: Release Conformance Organ
known_blockers: Ledger established at this baseline (Slice 25); the strict gate stays non-green until every organ below turns green and Slice 40 final release proof lands.

name: Constitutional Kernel
known_blockers: ConstitutionSnapshotV1 now exists (typed, versioned, digested, foundation laws immutable-by-validator) via aios.domain.governance.constitution, and a real decision path now rejects execution on a constitution-digest mismatch: CapabilityAuthority.consume() recomputes the live snapshot digest and refuses outright (CapabilityError) when it no longer matches the digest stamped on the capability's binding at issue time, with the digest now genuinely threaded Principal -> CapabilityBinding at all 6 real production construction sites and persisted through the CapabilityStore SQLite round-trip (a prior gap: the store had no constitution_digest column at all, so the stamped value was silently dropped before consume() could ever compare it). Still missing: durable cross-restart persistence/ratification of the constitution itself (separate from the Slice 37 amendment-authority machinery) and PolicyKernel still reads the old aios.policy.constitution.Constitution facade rather than ConstitutionSnapshotV1 -- a genuinely large, cross-cutting migration (every caste/routing/security check in the kernel depends on that facade's specific shape) intentionally out of scope for this pass.

name: Communication and Human-State Interpreter
known_blockers: Tier 3 update: classify_human_state() is now genuinely wired into the real, live /api/v1/chat turn path -- aios/application/turns/conversation_pipeline.py's stream_conversation() (the actual hot path, confirmed by reading it directly, not assumed) classifies every non-empty user turn, emits a new advisory 'human_state' SSE frame (always visible, user_correctable=True, grants_authority=False, before the route/text_chunk frames) and best-effort-persists it via a new HumanStateHypothesisStore (organ 30's own genuinely new append-only, digest-verified table -- migration 0010, scope human_representation). The persistence call site follows record_episode's own established convention exactly: no defensive try/except at the call site itself, the injected callable (_record_human_state) owns being best-effort, proven with a dedicated test that a broken store never raises. Still missing, honestly: the deterministic regex classifier itself has not been measured against real production operator traffic -- only synthetic labeled examples (tests/test_human_representation.py's parametrized cases) exist today, and no real usage history has accumulated yet in this sandbox to validate against; this is a genuine, not-fabricatable gap that closes naturally as real chat traffic accumulates in the new store.

name: Human Representative Context Compiler
known_blockers: Reconciliation note (this pass): this ledger row itself was stale -- the Tier 4 pass (commit 71ad30e) narrowed organ 31 in docs/architecture/GAGOS_54_ORGANS.md (exposing gateway_reasoning.py's previously-discarded context_digest as a real last_context_digest attribute) but never updated this JSON row, which still read the pre-Tier-4 'nothing calls this compiler yet' text -- a genuine drift between the doc and the ledger, caught and corrected here rather than perpetuated. This pass closes organ 31's durable-state gap: every RepresentativeContextV1 that passes identity/constitution/emergency-stop validation is now durably recorded by a new, append-only RepresentativeContextStore (migration 0011, scope 'intelligence'), wired directly inside route_intelligence_request()/stream_intelligence_request() (aios/application/intelligence/gateway.py) so every current and future gateway caller gets audit persistence for free, not just Council's. Tamper detection reuses the context's own canonical digest formula via a new context_digest_from_record() helper. Recording is best-effort (never blocks an already-governed call), matching _record_human_state()'s (organ 30) established convention. Still missing, honestly: the only real production caller today (GatewayRoutedCouncilLLMClient) compiles a context with empty active_preferences/project_passport/latest_correction -- Council's own prompts are self-contained JSON-instruction strings that don't need operator-preference injection, so this is a deliberate, not an accidental, gap. Meaningfully exercising the compiler with REAL human-representation data (not empty defaults) requires wiring it into a general-conversation call site (chat), which is organ 32's own named, deliberately-deferred gap -- see organ 32's blocker for why that wiring was not attempted in this pass either.

name: Universal Intelligence Gateway
known_blockers: Reconciliation pass item 2 (prompted by an explicit operator request to reconcile organs 40/41, which surfaced this as the natural next item): Council Planner/King LLM reasoning is now genuinely routed through route_intelligence_request() via aios/council/gateway_reasoning.py -- the first production wiring of route_intelligence_request() outside its own tests, and the first production wiring of Council's LLM slots at all (both PlannerQueen.__init__(llm=...) and CouncilOrchestrator's king_complete= were previously always None in every real construction site; grep-confirmed zero production hits before this). Every Council completion is now emergency-stop gated and produces a real, auditable context_digest. Investigated and fixed a real regression this surfaced: test_council_origination.py/test_royal_decree.py exercised this newly-live path with COUNCIL_REASONING=True and a real enrolled test operator, making a real ~11s Ollama call -- neutralized with config.COUNCIL_REASONING=False in those tests' setup, matching test_api.py's established FakeLLM-override convention for exactly this class of problem. Still not universal (this organ's own name): chat (/api/v1/chat) and the agentic forge (/api/generate) remain unwired -- both are STREAMING call sites (stream_chat_chunks/ToolAgent.run), while route_intelligence_request()'s model_call callback is synchronous request/response only. Wiring those would mean giving up token-by-token streaming unless the gateway grows a streaming variant first -- deliberately not attempted in this pass rather than accepting that UX regression silently. The two other competing gateway-shaped implementations (aios.runtime.intelligence_gateway.IntelligenceGateway, aios.application.models.hiring_service.IntelligenceHiringService) also remain unreconciled. Reconciliation note (this pass): organ 31's new RepresentativeContextStore is wired inside this organ's own module (aios/application/intelligence/gateway.py), so both existing entrances (route_intelligence_request()/stream_intelligence_request()) now durably record every context they compile, not just log-and-discard it -- a real, if modest, strengthening of this organ's own evidence trail. Chat (/api/v1/chat) and the agentic forge (/api/generate) remain genuinely unwired: chat's real hot-path call (aios/application/turns/conversation_pipeline.py::stream_conversation()) has no authenticated operator/constitution digest available today by design (anonymous local chat must keep working), and it is the single most heavily used endpoint in the whole system -- rewiring it without a live browser session to verify no UX regression was deliberately not attempted in this pass, consistent with every prior pass's own risk assessment of this exact call site. The two other competing gateway-shaped implementations (aios.runtime.intelligence_gateway.IntelligenceGateway, aios.application.models.hiring_service.IntelligenceHiringService, the latter confirmed load-bearing for real worker plan/repair reasoning and the hiring flow) also remain unreconciled -- not attempted here, same regression-risk reasoning as the operator's own prior scope decision.

name: Model Registry and Capability Passport
known_blockers: ModelPassportV1 now exists (typed, evidence-backed shape: qualified_roles/disallowed_roles/tool_protocol_status/admission_status) wrapping the existing aios.core.router.Provider pure-data model with the role-scoped admission concept it does not carry. is_admitted_for_role()/can_drive_tools()/is_stale_for_version() are real, deterministic checks. Still missing: nothing actually runs a qualification suite yet -- every ModelPassportV1 in this codebase must be hand-constructed; real qualification against the live granite3.2:2b Ollama model is Slice 32, and no durable store persists a passport across restarts.

Reconciliation pass item 4: the same live-registry drift documented in organ 37 lives here too -- ModelPassportV1's admission concept is real and typed, but the actual running LocalWorkforceRegistry (data/aios_memory.db, this machine's genuine operational state) is a separate, older, untyped admission store (local_worker_models table, allowed_job_profiles_json) that was found claiming an evidence-contradicted admission for granite3.2:2b -- corrected using the new aios/application/local_workforce/qualification_evidence.py (see organ 37). These two admission concepts (ModelPassportV1 vs. LocalWorkforceRegistry) still are not unified into one authority -- a real, separate architectural gap this item did not attempt to close.

name: Clerical Job Contract and Dispatcher
known_blockers: dispatch_clerical_job() now exists: deterministic code always wins first (Granite is never consulted when a parser can already answer), an unqualified or failed-qualification model always escalates to frontier regardless of self-reported confidence, and only a qualified model's own low-confidence result routes to a human. Still missing: nothing in aios.application.local_workforce.service.LocalWorkforceService (the real production run_advisory_job() path) calls this dispatcher yet -- it is a standalone, tested decision function, not wired into the live request path.

name: Durable Local-Clerk Provenance and Continuity Organ
known_blockers: Reconciliation pass item 3: the organ's own stated gap -- 'LocalWorkforceService.run_advisory_job() doesn't call this store yet, so no real job is persisted in production' -- is now resolved. run_advisory_job() is a thin wrapper around the original logic (renamed _execute_advisory_job, body byte-for-byte unchanged, zero validation-logic risk) that records every real job's request, model call, and result via LocalWorkforceProvenanceStore once execution completes -- honestly, including refusals (no admitted model) and schema-validation failures, not only successes. get_local_workforce_service() (aios/api/deps.py) now constructs and injects a real store at config.LOCAL_WORKFORCE_PROVENANCE_DB_PATH (new config constant), so this is genuinely live in the production DI path, not just available. Fail-safe by construction: provenance_store defaults to None and recording is skipped entirely when absent, matching every other optional-dependency pattern in this codebase. New tests/test_local_workforce_service_provenance.py (5 tests: no-store no-op, successful job recorded, rejection recorded, schema-failure recorded, two jobs don't collide). While grounding this, found and reported (not fixed here, flagged as a separate task) a real pre-existing bug unrelated to this change: three tests in test_r15_new_blockers.py's TestBlocker9LocalJobSchema silently never exercised the schema-validation code they claim to test, because a bare MagicMock() registry always satisfies hasattr(registry, 'dependency'), triggering a real-registry fallback that made every job 'rejected' rather than 'failed' -- their weak assertion (status != 'completed') passed either way. Still missing: dispatch_clerical_job() (Slice 32) itself remains unwired -- a full integration needs a QualificationResult tracked per model, which isn't stored anywhere in the registry today; inventing that under time pressure was deliberately not attempted here rather than forcing a shallow/fake integration.

live_evidence.description: CI run https://github.com/swap821/ai-editor/actions/runs/29890416231 (commit db0441144db9): a real docker-compose Executor Service container was started, and aios.application.governance.runtime_proof._probe_executor made a genuine HTTP call through StructuredExecutorClient to submit and execute an isolated job inside it -- verified real output confirming uid=65534 (non-root), network=blocked, and outside=blocked (no workspace escape). Separately, tests/test_executor_integration.py ran python -m pytest *inside* the real container via `docker compose run`, and test_missing_private_executor_is_refused proved there is no host-process fallback when the service is absent. This is genuine runtime evidence, not a fixture: reviewed the proof source directly (not just the passing CI job name) to confirm it makes real network calls against a real container rather than mocking the executor client.

CI run https://github.com/swap821/ai-editor/actions/runs/29989936411 (commit 697d925fa6c2151c4c59c2372ae98f411d5e74b2): the release-authority job's real docker-compose Executor Service container was RESTARTED in place (`docker compose restart executor` + `up -d --wait`, on the real GitHub Actions Docker daemon -- this local sandbox has none) and tests/test_executor_integration.py was re-run against it via a fresh `docker compose run`, genuinely passing all 3 tests post-restart -- the same real isolation proof (uid=65534, network=blocked, outside=blocked) as the original pre-restart run, now proven to survive a real container restart, not just a cold start. A first attempt at this same proof (run 29988647828, commit 51306512f09dee23ce54de6ff1bb39e0f7c155fb) genuinely FAILED and was root-caused, not glossed over: the restart step omitted re-exporting AIOS_DOCKER_SOCKET_GID, so the follow-up `docker compose up --wait` detected config drift against docker-compose.yml's silent `${AIOS_DOCKER_SOCKET_GID:-999}` fallback and recreated the container with the wrong docker-socket group, breaking its ability to spawn per-job containers -- fixed by exporting the same real GID the original start step already computes.

name: Recovery and Resumption
known_blockers: This pass closes the prerequisite the prior update named precisely: MissionService.request_approval_direct() (DRAFT -> AWAITING_APPROVAL via the pre-existing but previously-unused MissionTransition.DIRECT_REQUEST_APPROVAL) plus a real POST /api/v1/maintenance/repairs/{mission_id}/approve route now let a maintenance repair mission reach APPROVED through a real, privileged-operator-gated HTTP call -- tests/test_maintenance_api.py's own end-to-end test was rewritten to use this route instead of calling MissionService.approve() directly in-process, closing the exact bypass the prior update's own text named. A real, previously-latent bug was found and fixed while wiring this: three maintenance routes checked 'if record is None' against SqliteMissionRepository.get(), which raises MissionNotFoundError rather than returning None -- an unknown mission_id was an uncaught 500, not the intended 404; extracted a shared _get_mission_or_404() helper. Still missing: MissionTransitionJournal itself is not yet wired into MaintenanceConvergenceService.run_approved_repair() the way it already is into CouncilOrchestrator -- that wiring (11 states across ~6 internal steps) is now genuinely unblocked (the APPROVED-transition prerequisite this ledger previously named is closed) but was not attempted in the same pass as the prerequisite itself, to keep this an honest, single-purpose slice rather than two changes bundled together.

name: Local Skill Reuse, Confidence and Demotion
Golden Mission and Endurance Evaluation
known_blockers: The brief's golden release cohort (12 governed frontier missions across 2 real cloud providers, 4 candidate skills, 3 verified local reuses each, forced-refusal/version-drift-suspension/failed-reuse-demotion/human-revocation/cloud-outage/uncertain-escalation cases) requires hours of real governed mission execution against live frontier providers. Checked realistically rather than assumed: this is not achievable or appropriate to run autonomously in this environment/pass -- it needs real cloud credentials, real mission budgets, and genuine multi-hour wall-clock execution, not something to simulate or fake. Recorded honestly as not attempted, not silently skipped. The individual mechanisms this cohort would exercise (demotion, revocation, suspension) are real and unit-tested in organ 43; what's missing is the live, expensive, end-to-end proof run itself.

name: Constitutional Amendment Authority
known_blockers: This pass closes the prior update's own named gap: activate_amendment_route() previously rebuilt a fresh 'previous' snapshot via build_constitution_snapshot() on every single call with no prior snapshot ever passed in, so every activation looked like the very first one (always version 1 -> 2) and nothing was ever persisted for rollback_amendment() to find 'the exact predecessor' of. New ConstitutionSnapshotStore (migration 0012, scope 'constitution') is content-addressed by snapshot_digest with a separate current-pointer table per constitution_id, so a rollback re-points at an existing row instead of duplicating it. activate_amendment_route() now reads the real current snapshot from the store (building/persisting a version-1 baseline only the first time) and persists every activation's result, so successive amendments genuinely chain -- proven with a real two-activation HTTP test asserting the second digest differs from the first. The new POST .../amendments/{proposal_id}/rollback route wires the pre-existing rollback_amendment() domain function to this real history; CONSTITUTIONAL_AMENDMENT_ROLLBACK already existed as an unused ActionType (the same dead-action-type pattern organ 42's DIRECT_REQUEST_APPROVAL had) -- it now has a real YELLOW, confirm-required route mirroring ratify/activate's established two-phase pattern, proven with 4 new tests (happy path, unratified refusal, unknown proposal 404, no-history refusal). Named honestly, not silently: ConstitutionalAmendmentProposalV1 does not itself record which snapshot digest its own activation produced, so rollback reverts the operator's current constitution to its immediate predecessor, not necessarily undoing precisely the named proposal_id if another amendment activated since -- rollback_amendment()'s pre-existing contract already has this shape; this change does not weaken or hide it further, and extending ConstitutionalAmendmentProposalV1's own schema to close that precision gap is real, separate follow-up scope.

name: Constitutional Learning Organ
known_blockers: Tier 3 update: the HTTP surface now exists, reusing organ 45's just-established pattern exactly. 3 new ActionType members (CONSTITUTIONAL_LESSON_PROPOSE/DRAFT_AMENDMENT/CHECK_SIMULATIONS) plus real RouteAuthority entries. New routes in aios/api/routes/governance.py: POST .../lessons/propose, GET .../lessons/{id} + .../history, POST .../lessons/{id}/draft-amendment (calls the real lesson_to_amendment_proposal(), persists BOTH the updated lesson and the resulting real ConstitutionalAmendmentProposalV1 via GovernanceAmendmentStore -- the drafted proposal is then independently fetchable and ratifiable through organ 45's own routes, proven end to end in tests), and POST .../lessons/check-simulations (a pure, stateless gate over require_all_simulations_pass() -- this organ still does not run the 9 named adversarial simulations itself, only validates results a caller already produced, refusing on any missing or failed check). 8 new HTTP tests, including a genuine authority-reduction refusal at draft-amendment time and both a missing-simulation and a failed-simulation refusal. Organ 45's capability-issuance-table gap this organ's blocker previously named is now resolved (see organ 45's own closure). Still missing (unchanged scope, documented by constitutional_learning.py's own docstring as deliberate): the 9 named adversarial simulations remain a fixed catalog this module checks results FOR, not simulations it runs -- a genuine, not-fabricatable gap (real adversarial testing against a running system), not a wiring gap.

name: Observability and Health Organ
known_blockers: This pass closes the Docker executor boundary the prior update named as genuinely separate, not-yet-attempted work. Three real hops now carry the same correlation ids, each tested in isolation: (1) StructuredExecutorClient.execute() sends get_trace_context().headers() on its HTTP request into the executor service -- previously sent nothing but Accept/Authorization/Content-Type; (2) aios/executor_service.py's execute_job() reads those incoming headers and binds a TraceContext for the duration of the job's dispatch; (3) aios.core.executor.DockerRunner.__call__ (the subprocess.Popen boundary the prior update named) now reads get_trace_context() and adds it as fixed --env entries in the spawned container's argv, deliberately kept separate from the job's own security-reviewed environment_allowlist mechanism, which this must not bypass or widen. New TraceContext.as_env() reshapes the same non-empty header values into env-var names (AIOS_TRACE_REQUEST_ID, etc.) for the subprocess hop, distinct from headers() used for the HTTP hop. Still missing, honestly: this sandbox has no live Docker daemon (confirmed via docker ps failing), so the full chain is proven correct at each hop via injected fakes/spies (matching this file's own established process_runner override pattern), not via one real end-to-end run through an actual spawned container -- that live confirmation is the same category of gap organ 40 named and later closed once CI's own Docker-enabled runner ran it, not something this sandbox can produce on its own.

name: Installation, Configuration and Key Authority
known_blockers: Key rotation and a bounded grace period are now real (operator-confirmed design: grace-period overlap, not an immediate hard cutover). New ApiTokenAuthority (aios/application/security/api_token_authority.py) issues a fresh API bearer token via a new POST /api/v1/security/api-token/rotate route; the token it supersedes keeps working for a caller-chosen grace period (default 3600s) so an already-running client is not broken the instant rotation happens, then stops validating once the window elapses -- proven with a fake-clock unit test, not just asserted. config.API_TOKEN (the env-configured value) stays unconditionally valid exactly as before this organ existed -- the operator retires it the normal way, by restarting with a different env var; this authority only owns tokens issued through an explicit rotate() call, layered on top, so every pre-existing test and behavior around the static token is unchanged. A real regression was caught and fixed before shipping: an early draft cached config.API_TOKEN's value inside the long-lived authority singleton at first construction, so a test elsewhere in the suite that temporarily reassigns config.API_TOKEN would permanently poison every later, unrelated test in the same process -- caught by real adversarial-suite regressions (tests/adversarial/test_api_security.py), not by a test written for this change; fixed by making the authority stateless with respect to config.API_TOKEN (every method takes it fresh as an argument) rather than caching it. Still missing: 'truthful Ollama-absence handling' -- the other named half of this organ's original blocker -- has no further specification anywhere in the repo beyond that one paraphrased sentence, and is an unrelated concern (local-model availability reporting, not credential rotation); not investigated or built in this pass.
